static.description: A polyglot microservices platform for food delivery, built with 5 independent services (API Gateway, Order, Inventory, Driver, Notification) across Kotlin, Java, Python, Go, and TypeScript stacks, communicating via RabbitMQ events and fronted by a JWT-authenticated API Gateway.
domain_concepts: OrderStatus: Enum representing the lifecycle states of an order: PENDING → ACCEPTED → PREPARING → READY_FOR_PICKUP → IN_TRANSIT → DELIVERED, with CANCELLED and REFUNDED as terminal/side states.
OrderItem: An embeddable JPA entity representing a line item within an order, containing menu item ID, name, quantity, and unit price.
MenuItem: A SQLAlchemy model representing a restaurant menu item with stock_quantity (-1 means unlimited) and availability tracking.
Driver: A MongoDB document representing a delivery driver with name, phone, vehicle type, availability status, and GPS location.
GeoPoint: A struct/document embedded in Driver storing latitude and longitude coordinates for real-time location tracking.
JwtAuthFilter: A Spring Cloud Gateway global filter that validates JWT tokens and forwards the verified customer ID as an X-Customer-Id header to downstream services.
acquire_lock: A Redis-based distributed lock function in the Inventory Service that prevents concurrent stock overselling by acquiring an exclusive lock on a menu item.
order.events: The RabbitMQ exchange name used for publishing order lifecycle events that the Notification Service consumes.
stripePaymentIntentId: The Stripe PaymentIntent ID (pi_xxxx) stored on an Order, used for manual capture and refund operations.
Restaurant: A SQLAlchemy model representing a restaurant with name, address, cuisine type, rating, and associated menu items.
isValidTransition: A static method on OrderStatus that enforces the allowed state machine transitions for order lifecycle.

dynamic.test_framework: JUnit 5 (Order Service), pytest (Inventory Service), Go testing (Driver Service), Jest (Notification Service)
dynamic.architecture_summary: A polyglot microservices architecture with an API Gateway (Kotlin/Spring Cloud Gateway) performing JWT authentication and routing requests to Order Service (Java/Spring Boot), Inventory Service (Python/FastAPI), and Driver Service (Go). Asynchronous order lifecycle events are published via RabbitMQ and consumed by a Notification Service (Node.js/TypeScript) that dispatches SMS (Twilio) and email (SendGrid). A React/TypeScript frontend communicates with services through the API Gateway.

key_components.name: API Gateway
key_components.responsibility: Routes all incoming HTTP requests, performs JWT authentication, and forwards verified customer ID to downstream services via X-Customer-Id header.

key_components.name: JWT Auth Filter
key_components.responsibility: Global gateway filter that validates JWT tokens, extracts the subject claim, and rejects unauthenticated requests to non-public paths.

key_components.name: Order Service
key_components.responsibility: Manages the full order lifecycle including creation, status transitions, cancellation, and refunds, with Stripe payment integration and RabbitMQ event publishing.

key_components.name: Order Controller
key_components.responsibility: REST API endpoints for creating, listing, retrieving, updating status, and cancelling orders.

key_components.name: Order Service Logic
key_components.responsibility: Business logic for order operations including payment intent creation, status validation, event publishing to RabbitMQ, and refund processing.

key_components.name: Order Model
key_components.responsibility: JPA entity representing an order with embedded line items, Stripe payment intent tracking, and lifecycle timestamps.

key_components.name: OrderStatus
key_components.responsibility: Enum defining valid order states and transition rules for the order lifecycle state machine.

key_components.name: Stripe Payment Gateway
key_components.responsibility: Live Stripe SDK integration for creating payment intents with manual capture and processing refunds.

key_components.name: Inventory Service
key_components.responsibility: FastAPI application managing restaurants and menu items with PostgreSQL, using Redis distributed locks for safe stock reservation under concurrent access.

key_components.name: Inventory Models
key_components.responsibility: SQLAlchemy ORM models for Restaurant and MenuItem entities with support for unlimited vs finite stock tracking.

key_components.name: Driver Service
key_components.responsibility: Go HTTP service managing driver profiles, availability status, and real-time GPS location updates backed by MongoDB.

key_components.name: Notification Service
key_components.responsibility: Consumes RabbitMQ order events and dispatches SMS (Twilio) and email (SendGrid) notifications with in-process idempotency tracking.

key_components.name: Frontend App
key_components.responsibility: React SPA with role-based views (Customer, Restaurant, Driver, Admin) providing a complete UI for the food delivery platform.

key_components.name: Frontend API Client
key_components.responsibility: Axios HTTP client configured with base URL pointing to the API Gateway and automatic JWT Bearer token injection.

key_components.name: Auth Context
key_components.responsibility: React context providing JWT token management with localStorage persistence and JWT subject claim extraction for customer ID.

key_components.name: Frontend Types
key_components.responsibility: TypeScript type definitions for all domain entities shared across frontend components and API modules.

agent_safe_operations: Adding new REST endpoints to existing services following established patterns
Adding new React components or views in the frontend
Adding unit tests to any service's test directory
Modifying business logic within individual service source files
Adding new API client functions in frontend/src/api/
Adding new TypeScript types in frontend/src/types/index.ts
Updating inventory-service models.py to add new database columns
Adding new routes to the Driver Service handler
Adding new order status transition rules in OrderStatus.java
Updating frontend component styling with Tailwind classes
agent_forbidden_operations: Modifying .env.example with real secrets or credentials
Changing CI workflow files without explicit approval
Modifying the openapi.yaml specification without corresponding code changes
Changing JWT secret handling or authentication filter bypass logic
Removing or weakening the DriverStore/PaymentGateway interface abstractions
Hardcoding credentials, API keys, or secrets in any source file
Changing database migration strategies or dropping tables
Altering RabbitMQ exchange names or routing key patterns without updating all consumers
potential_pitfalls: Each service uses a different language/framework/build system — agents must detect which service context they're working in before running commands.
The Order Service requires a running PostgreSQL and RabbitMQ for integration tests; the driver-service integration tests need MongoDB (or use mock store).
The Inventory Service uses async SQLAlchemy (asyncpg) — standard synchronous SQLAlchemy patterns will fail.
Redis distributed locks in the Inventory Service have retry logic with specific TTL — modifying lock parameters can cause race conditions or deadlocks.
The API Gateway JWT filter requires a secret of sufficient length for HMAC-SHA — short secrets will cause cryptographic errors at runtime.
OrderStatus.isValidTransition enforces a strict state machine — adding new statuses requires updating all transition rules.
The frontend LoginView generates dev JWTs client-side with a hardcoded secret (DEV_SECRET) that must match the gateway's JWT_SECRET for local development.
The frontend API client hardcodes baseURL to localhost:8080 — this must be the gateway port, not individual service ports.
The notification service uses an in-memory Set for idempotency — this does not survive restarts and won't work with multiple replicas.
The docker-compose.yml appears truncated in the repository — the inventory-service environment section may be incomplete.
Go service tests use both unit tests (mockStore + httptest.NewRequest) and integration tests (httptest.NewServer) — they live in the same package but serve different purposes.
The PaymentGateway interface is critical for testability — replacing it with direct Stripe calls will break the test architecture.
Python inventory-service uses pydantic_settings (v2) not pydantic (v1) — import paths differ between versions.
